figures.label: Figure 4.5
figures.caption: represent opportunities for breach of the closed integrity of the bioreactor system. The closure analysis should consider these steps and determine whether the methodology used in the step or subsequent steps adequately protects the integrity of the closed system. Stainless steel bioreactor systems require significant manual intervention during setup. The insertion of instrumentation into lower probe belts and assembly of vent and sterile gas filters can result in particulate and bioburden contamination of the vessel and system. The risk may be adequately mitigated by performing SIP on the system prior to use.2 A hot WFI rinsing and sanitizing rinse after manual insertions, but prior to SIP, may be needed to mitigate the risk of particulate contamination within the vessel chamber. Bioburden present in the system after human intervention is usually removed using thermal sanitization

figures.label: Figure 4.12
figures.caption: , the lead filter no longer represents the sterile barrier and is no more than a very expensive pre-filter. In addition, the SIP of the lead filter actually increases the risk of the overall operation. A more cost effective and lower risk option is to use a non-absolute 0.2 µm filter that is not steamed in place during the setup of the system.

figures.label: Figure 1.0-1
figures.caption: Identifying Whether an Excipient is

figures.label: Figure 4.1.1-1
figures.caption: is one of the lowest-risk arrangements due to the small number of intermediaries and the degree of control that can be applied.

figures.label: Figure 4.1.2-1
figures.caption: , presents few more risks than the simple manufacturer-to-end user scenario; however, those risks may be magnified if the distributor engages in repacking or relabeling, as discussed in Section 4.1.3.

figures.label: Figure 4.1.3-1
figures.caption: , magnify the risks to excipi- ent quality. The end user needs to understand the GMP controls applied by the repackager in order to minimize the risks to excipient quality, and, if feasible, needs to specify appropriate measures in supply or quality agreements.

figures.label: Figure 4.1.4-1
figures.caption: depicts a possible rela- tionship between a distributor and the end user when a broker is involved.

figures.label: Figure 4.1.5-1
figures.caption: usually involves many in- termediaries. Unlike previous examples in which the parties are relatively fixed, the parties to an in- ternational supply chain, including haulers, shipping agents, and ships, may vary from shipment to shipment. In these situations, quantifying the risks may be very difficult. If the intermediaries are fixed, however, suitable inspection controls can be applied at those points, reducing the uncertainty presented by the prior steps.

figures.label: Figure 6.0-1
figures.caption: illustrates the steps in the process. In other sections below, this TR highlights and discusses portions of this model.

figures.label: Figure 6.3-1
figures.caption: focuses in on one section of Figure 6.0-1 to illustrate the initiation of the risk assessment when an excipient is needed for a product.

figures.label: Figure 6.3.1-1.
figures.caption: A column marked “Compliance” is included near the CQAs so that compliance with, for instance, pharmacopeial specifications, can be ensured as well. This column is useful because, in some cases, an attribute may be part of a specification, e.g., a pharmacopeial specification, that the company will claim adherence to, regardless of the actual impact on the drug product CQAs. This column allows the assessors to force such excipient attributes further into the assessment and into the excipient specification.

figures.label: Figure 6.4-1
figures.caption: Steps in the Supply Chain Risk Analysis

figures.label: Figure 6.5-1
figures.caption: ), based on the excipient risk score (ERPN) on one side and the supply chain risk

figures.label: Figure 6.5.2-1
figures.caption: , where n is the sum of the weights in the numerator. If the excipient risk score is given a weight of 2 and the supply chain risk score is given the weight of 1, then n = 3.

figures.label: Figure 6.6-1
figures.caption: Identify Further Mitigation Actions to Reach Acceptable Risk Control, and Communicate

figures.label: Figure 6.6.1-1
figures.caption: illustrates the extent and degree of risk mitigation that could be required based on the type of drug product and its use. This mitigation is defined by the excipient, how it is made, how it is supplied, why it is used, and how it functions in the drug product, as determined in the risk assess- ment. The degree of mitigation, that is, the reduction in overall risk that is necessary to reach a low residual risk level, increases as the different definitions of GMP are applied. The list of QMS standards and GMP definitions provided here is illustrative of the principle and is designed to help identify the GMP definition that reduces the risk to the desired level.

figures.label: Figure 7.0-1
figures.caption: Simplified Overview of the Generic Lifecycle Model

figures.label: Figure 10.3-1.
figures.caption: In this way, the company evaluates the impact of the excipient on the finished drug products. Attributes are derived by ideation from a defined list of potential risk sources. Attributes with impact are included on the specification, and limits or other controls are defined after analyzing the possible impacts of variation in the attribute. This method uses a qualitative assessment with a simple +/- score for impact combined with text rationales and references describing the underlying science. The impact is evaluated considering the amount of material to be used and its function in the formulation.

figures.label: Table 6.1.4.
figures.caption: The level of effort and formality required for each product assessment depends on the level of risk identified. These examples are not all-inclusive. Organizations should evaluate and determine which strategies for drug shortage control will be the most effective for the risks identified for their specific product and its use. Where appropriate, these strategies should be documented in procedures, integrated into the quality system and/or business processes, imple- mented in a consistent manner across the company, and actively managed using a Drug Shortage Risk Register. Section 11.0: Appendix 1 provides a template for creating a Drug Shortage Risk Register.

figures.label: Figure 4.4.3-1
figures.caption: ) is used to incubate the roller bottles. The room is maintained at 36 °C ± 1 °C. Activities in this room include introducing roller bottle racks, rotating roller bottles, removing roller bottles from the room, and inspecting daily for gross contamination or leaks of roller bottles; roller bottles will not be touched by humans. Due to the high traffic in the room and the higher than ambient temperature, sites in this room will be sampled weekly. Protecting the cleanli- ness of this room is key to the strategy of not contaminating roller bottles.
